tldr: Bonsai is managed Elasticsearch/OpenSearch hosting; SerpApi is a hosted search-engine results (SERP) scraping API for Google, Bing, etc. They are not drop-in replacements. Migrate only if your real need is third-party SERP data rather than indexing your own content. Otherwise pick a different target (e.g. Algolia, Typesense, Meilisearch, OpenSearch Service).
why_switch: Need live Google/Bing/Yahoo/DuckDuckGo result data instead of indexing your own corpus
Want to drop the ops cost of running and tuning Elasticsearch indexes
Use case is SEO monitoring, rank tracking, or competitive research rather than site search
Prefer a pure usage-based REST API with no cluster sizing, shards, or backups to manage
Bonsai costs scale faster than expected once free-tier limits are hit
what_to_export: List of indexes and their mappings (field types, analyzers)
Index settings (shards, replicas, custom analyzers, synonyms)
Saved query templates and search application configs
Ingest pipelines and any scripted fields
API keys, role mappings, and IP allowlists
Dashboards/visualizations if Kibana/OpenSearch Dashboards is used
export_method: Use Elasticsearch/OpenSearch native APIs against your Bonsai cluster URL: GET /_mapping and GET /_settings for schema, the snapshot API or elasticdump for documents, and copy auth/network config from the Bonsai dashboard. Bonsai provides standard ES-compatible endpoints, so any ES client works.
import_method: SerpApi has no index to import into — it queries external search engines on demand. 'Migration' means rewriting your application code: replace Elasticsearch queries with SerpApi HTTP calls (engine, q, location, and other params per the SerpApi docs) and parse the structured JSON response. If you still need to search your own content, stand up a separate index (OpenSearch, Algolia, Typesense, Meilisearch) and only use SerpApi for external SERP data.

concept_mapping.note: Bonsai indexes hold your data; SerpApi engines target a public search engine

concept_mapping.target: Organic result item in JSON response
concept_mapping.note: You do not own the documents on SerpApi

concept_mapping.target: Query string params (q, location, hl, gl, num)
concept_mapping.note: No DSL — relevance is controlled by the upstream engine
No schema; results are whatever the SERP returns
Capacity is measured in API calls, not nodes
Single account-level key for all engines
You must persist responses yourself if you need history

effort: 1-3 weeks if the use case truly is SERP data; otherwise treat as a re-architecture and budget 4-8+ weeks because you also need a new search backend.
gotchas: SerpApi is not a search index — you cannot store or search your own documents on it
Pricing is per successful API call; bursty traffic can spike costs unlike Bonsai's flat tiers
Result schemas differ per engine (google vs bing vs yahoo) — write engine-specific parsers
Rate limits and concurrent-request limits depend on plan; build retry/backoff
SERP results change constantly; cache responses if you need stable data for tests or analytics
Legal/compliance: confirm you may store and redistribute scraped SERP content for your use case

email.subject: Moving off Bonsai — checking if SerpApi fits
email.body:
Hi team,

We're evaluating leaving Bonsai (managed Elasticsearch). Before we commit to SerpApi, I want to flag that the two products solve different problems: Bonsai indexes our own content, SerpApi returns live results from Google/Bing/etc. If our real need is searching internal data, SerpApi alone won't replace Bonsai and we should also pick an indexed-search target (Algolia, Typesense, Meilisearch, or self-hosted OpenSearch).

Proposed steps:
1. Snapshot all Bonsai indexes (mappings, settings, documents) via the standard ES APIs.
2. Classify each query: 'our data' vs 'public SERP data'.
3. Move public-SERP queries to SerpApi; route internal-search queries to a new indexed backend.
4. Run both stacks in parallel for one billing cycle, then cut over and decommission Bonsai.

Let me know if you want me to scope option B (single indexed-search target) instead.

Thanks
